column1: # A: Indexes
Responses: 1–20 integer scales.
Strict Definition: Discrete Ordinal. You are collecting whole numbers (discrete) that have a meaningful rank order (ordinal)
column2: but the true psychological distance between a '4' and a '5' might not be exactly the same as between a '19' and a '20'.

column1: Practical Analysis Treatment: Interval / Quantitative. In HCI literature
column2: 20-point scales are almost universally treated as Continuous/Interval data. When you calculate the overall RTLX score (the sum or average of the 6 dimensions)
column3: you will treat that final number as a continuous quantitative variable
column4: which allows you to use parametric tests like repeated-measures ANOVAs or paired t-tests (assuming normal distribution).

# B: Statements
Responses: 1–5 (Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree).
Strict Definition: Categorical Ordinal. A 5-point Likert scale is the textbook definition of ordinal data. The categories have a strict logical order
column2: but they are qualitative labels translated into numbers.

column1: Practical Analysis Treatment:
Item-Level: If you analyze a single question (e.g.
column2: "I thought the system was easy to use")
column3: it remains Ordinal. You should use medians/modes and non-parametric tests (like the Wilcoxon signed-rank test).

# C: Questions
Responses: Three text fields.
Strict Definition: Qualitative Unstructured. Free-text responses do not have numerical value or inherent rank.
Practical Analysis Treatment: Categorical / Nominal. To analyze this scientifically
